title: AWS Security & Governance Toolkit
description:
## Securing and Governing an AWS Environment

At the SAP-C02 level you are expected to assemble the right combination of AWS security services rather than rely on a single control. Security on AWS follows the **shared responsibility model**: AWS secures the cloud (hardware, hypervisor, managed-service internals); you secure what you put *in* the cloud (identities, data, configuration, network exposure). Effective designs layer **identity**, **encryption**, **detection**, **audit**, and **edge protection**.

### The core services

| Service | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **IAM** | Defines **identities** (users, groups, roles) and **policies** (JSON permission documents). Roles + temporary credentials (STS) are the preferred way to grant access to workloads and federated/human users. |
| **AWS Organizations + SCPs** | Multi-account governance. **Service Control Policies** set the maximum permissions any principal in an account/OU can have (a guardrail, not a grant). |
| **KMS** | Managed **encryption keys**. Customer managed keys (CMKs) give you control over key policies, rotation, and audit; used for envelope encryption across S3, EBS, RDS, etc. |
| **Secrets Manager** | Stores **secrets** (DB credentials, API keys) with built-in **automatic rotation** and fine-grained IAM access. |
| **SSM Parameter Store** | Hierarchical config + `SecureString` secrets (KMS-encrypted). Cheaper than Secrets Manager but **no native rotation**. |
| **GuardDuty** | Agentless **threat detection** — analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs with ML/threat intel to flag malicious behavior (crypto-mining, credential exfiltration, recon). |
| **Amazon Inspector** | Automated **vulnerability assessment** of EC2, ECR images, and Lambda (CVEs, network reachability). |
| **Security Hub** | **Aggregates findings** from GuardDuty, Inspector, Config, Macie, and partners; runs automated security standards (CIS, AWS FSBP) and gives a single posture dashboard. |
| **AWS Config** | Records **configuration state and history** of resources, evaluates them against **compliance rules**, and shows drift over time. |
| **CloudTrail** | **API audit log** — records who did what, when, from where, across the account/organization. The forensic source of truth. |
| **Amazon Cognito** | **Application end-user** identity (sign-up/sign-in, MFA, federation). Distinct from IAM, which is for AWS API/console access. |
| **WAF** | Layer-7 web **firewall** — filters HTTP(S) on CloudFront/ALB/API Gateway (SQLi, XSS, rate limiting, bot control). |
| **Shield (Standard/Advanced)** | **DDoS protection** at the edge; Advanced adds cost protection and a response team. |

### How they fit together

```
Identity      -> IAM, Organizations/SCPs, Cognito (app users)
Data           -> KMS, Secrets Manager, Parameter Store, Macie
Detection      -> GuardDuty, Inspector, Security Hub
Audit/Config   -> CloudTrail, AWS Config
Edge           -> WAF, Shield, CloudFront
```

### Key mental models

> **IAM grants, SCPs cap, permission boundaries delegate-safely.** A principal's effective permissions are the *intersection* of its identity policy, any permission boundary, the SCPs above it, and (for cross-account) the resource policy — minus any explicit Deny, which always wins.

> **Detect vs. assess vs. audit.** GuardDuty detects *behavior* (threats happening now), Inspector assesses *vulnerabilities* (weaknesses that could be exploited), CloudTrail/Config record *what happened/what exists* for audit and compliance.

When this lesson makes sense, mark it complete and tackle the scenario questions that follow.
